video_title: TITAN: The OceanGate Submersible Disaster | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I'm writing to you with a perspective I hope you'll consider as you move forward with the OceanGate project. Like many others, I've been following the story closely, and I'm eager to see how you approach such a complex and sensitive topic.

It's understandable that much of the current narrative focuses on Stockton Rush and his role in the Titan's failure. However, I'm concerned that the narrative is easily leaning towards solely blaming him because he is no longer here to offer his perspective or defend his decisions. It feels, at times, like all fingers are pointed to a single point because it's the easiest path. Instead he has opened the gate of the ocean for humankind.

My hope is that the film will strive for a more nuanced understanding of the circumstances leading up to the tragedy. It's vital to explore the broader context, including any potential systemic pressures, regulatory gaps, the nature of the industry, or other contributing factors that might have played a role, and to do so without the convenience of having a sole individual to blame.

Ultimately, I believe a more comprehensive and thoughtful approach will lead to a more compelling and impactful film that honors the lives lost and prevents similar tragedies in the future.

comment_text: I just finished watching this. I can’t help myself but to find analogy between Rush actions and German pilot who intentionally flied the airplane full of people into the mountain wall of the Alps. Both knew very well how their actions would end. Rush was on a suicidal or more precisely on the serial killer mission of getting the fame he so craved. He needed the publicity and the money to go on and he was willing to scarifies people lives for it. No amount of expert opinion or data could avert him, he would carry on no matter what. I don’t care if the submarine didn’t belong to the US but he was a US citizen and he committed criminal actions. The families deserve to get justice and compensation like it was an aircraft disaster. Rush didn’t have the right to make the victims to sign away their lives, as the victims were not given the necessary information to make informed consent.

I just, about an hour ago, finished watching this documentary. I don't use the word "hate" very often and reserve it for the worst of the worst of experiences of my existence. However, a common theme seems to be reoccurring whenever I catch myself uttering that word. I have uttered it several times already this past six months of 2025. I hate narcissistic billionaires, CEOs and just about any leader with that trait.

Narcissistic billionaires wonton disregard for human life and to be held accountable for anything has become insultingly commonplace. Their existence, via Forbes, has been a net negative, an absolute blight, worse than any natural disaster that humankind has ever experienced. Wherever and whenever they exist, expect to find hidden behind NDAs and abandoned lawsuits, a trail of death, human suffering and preventable tragedies. Nothing in our laws or body politic can hold them accountable. They even get to flaunting their toy Supreme Court Justices in our faces, whenever we attempt to amend laws to reign them in. God help us if one of them ever becomes President.

video_title: TITAN: The OceanGate Submersible Disaster | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I watched the Netflix documentary, and it was fairly good, but just like other shows about the OceanGate debacle, there was little or no mention about the amateurish methods of putting together the carbon fiber hull (it was wound like a spool of thread), nor the fact that Stockton Rush bought prepreg carbon fiber that was past its expiration date.  In my opinion there should have been some mention of the issue of Stockton Rush's childlike fixation on carbon fiber without him researching how to put carbon fiber together, and what its limitations were, in addition to the risks of putting two dissimilar materials (carbon fiber and titanium) together that would have different expansion and contraction properties.  I think there should've been more focus on the ridiculous "engineering" that was doomed to fail because anyone with any knowlege of carbon fiber would know that Stockton Rush's methods could not work.  And why didn’t the documentary dig in a little deeper about the other folks who assisted Stockton Rush with the ridiculous spool-winding of the carbon fiber hull?  It was akin to a child trying to make a cardboard space rocket.  Just because the cardboard is thick enough to work for a minute, doesn’t mean its safe or long lasting.  I think this Netflix documentary had a chance to point out every bit of lunacy in OceanGate's methodologies, but failed to do so.  It was as if Netflix was being too careful, and was trying to not overly criticize OceanGate.

comment_text: A very good investigative documentary that charts the full history of Stockton's rise and intensely hubris-charged fall. A bit more elaboration on the actual disaster would have made it better, but guess that's pending the US Coast Guard report. Interesting that the only Brit employee of OceanGate tried to whistleblow to OSHA a year in advance of the implosion, but they were asleep on the job. Surely OSHA bear some culpability for not recognising the imminent threat to the public? It shouldn't have been for a private individual to bear crippling court costs in a matter concerning public safety.

video_title: TITAN: The OceanGate Submersible Disaster | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: A real shame, I had hoped for more details on the failure of the sub. Instead, the documentary was primarily about Stockton Rush. Sure, that's also fascinating, but as an engineer I would have been much more interested in how it ultimately came to failure. The wreckage suggests that it was not the carbon part itself that broke, but rather the adhesive connection to the titanium flange.

I feel so bad for the families of the passengers that died and I find it honestly really disrespectful that they put his name right next to the others at the memorial at the Titanic Museum. He wasn’t a victim he was the one that took them to their deaths he literally said in one of the videos I saw I’m going to dive, even if it kills me so clearly living was not on his agenda, but it certainly was for those poor souls that had to die because of his negligence. I’m just imagining that they managed to find the only intact piece of equipment of the disaster being the IKEA grade game controller

comment_text:
LOL, the guy calling Rush a psychopath is the same one that under oath admitted he was the person who convinced Rush to fire all the outside engineers who were trying to lead Rush in the right direction.  And that same guy was the "Engineering Director" who spent years designing this submersible right beside Stockton Rush, without getting fired. 

Stockton and Nissen designed the first hull after Nissen successfully got all the competing outside certified Professional Engineers fired from the project. And that hull never made it to depth prior to failing, after which Nissen was fired. He wasn't fired because "I told Stockton he couldn't take the hull down with paying passengers".  It was freaking obvious that hull wasn't going to take people down - it had failed. Cracked. Effectively destroyed. It didn't take an engineer to tell Stockton that hull was a goner. 

The second hull, after Nissen was fired, made it to the Titanic 13 times. I

I'm not defending the Titan or Rush in any way, but it's just maddening that other people who helped him, encouraged him, and especially the one who admitted to having convinced Rush to FIRE the outside certified Professional Engineers - is being interviewed as some kind of expert here?

comment_text: So on June 18th 2023 at between 10:47AM and 10:48AM , at a depth of 3346m, the 'Titan' sub, on its final decent to the wreck of the ' 'TITANIC' operating under the company OCEANGATE (along with its founder Stockton Rush) sent its final ping to the mother ship of the expedition (MV 'Polar Prince' off the coast of Newfoundland Canada), losing all five hands on board. This June 18th 2025 will mark the 2 year anniversary of the era-defining event. It should remind us all that undue loss in the pursuits of scientific and human progress should never be allowed to occur on a planet that prides itself on personal and individual freedoms. No dream should ever incorporate or involve those parties not expressly and fully responsible for the success of a scientific experiment, and there is no excuse worthy of this sort of blind ambition, and it should be discouraged and prohibited at all costs.

video_title: TITAN: The OceanGate Submersible Disaster | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
let me break it down for you on how exactly they died... 

in simple words:
have u guys seen hydraulic press videos on youtube....right!! 
So in this titan case.. where it went, deep down to see that ship & body of leonardo dicaprio and that stupid old lady who threw jewel in the sea....i mean cmonnn guys... the water pressure is immense down there like over 5,500 pounds per square inch (PSI)

its like a hydraulic press, but way more powerful and from every direction at once. Imagine a hydraulic press crushing a soda can.... that’s similar to what happens when a sub like Titan implodes under deep sea pressure but instead of slow crushing, the ocean’s pressure acts instantly!!!

It crushes the vessel in all directions, 
not just top down..... just think of it as the ocean being one giant invisible hydraulic press, always pushing in. If the sub's structure can’t take it, it collapses in nano seconds like in an instant.... no chance to escape, no warning, just total destruction.
....
so the point is: never poke your fingers in nature's hydraulic press :)

video_title: TITAN: The OceanGate Submersible Disaster | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: My weird titan coincidence: I was literally writing a research paper on the sinking and wreck of the Titanic when this submersible went missing. I had no idea, found out later. Also I'm from Karachi, Pakistan. Two of the people on the submersible were from here. Just a month before the disaster I had visited a Science Centre built by them! 💔 It's called "TDF Magnifi Science Centre" look at the photos/videos and see for yourself how truly passionate they were about science and exploration. The Dawood family is very philanthropic and have built many schools, universities, hospitals etc. They weren't just "rich guys" looking for a thrill. They were genuinely interested in titanic/deep sea exploration.
